records.title: Positive airway pressure initiation is associated with improved lung function in persons with cystic fibrosis
records.summary: Objectives: It is uncertain if treating sleep breathing disorders in the setting of cystic fibrosis (CF) improves pulmonary outcomes. Among persons with CF, our purpose was to evaluate the relationship between incident positive airway pressure (PAP) receipt and respiratory-related hospitalizations and lung function trajectory. Methods: This retrospective, data-linkage, population-based, self-controlled interval risk study linked data from the Canadian CF Registry and Ontario health administrative databases. All persons with CF newly receiving PAP between January 1, 2009-December 31, 2020, who remained alive and did not receive lung transplantation during follow-up, were included. Rate of respiratory-related ho...
records.why_it_matters: Positive airway pressure may improve lung-function trajectory in selected CF patients with sleep breathing disorders.
records.confidence_reason: Linked data support the finding, but subgroup sizes are modest and hospitalization effects were nonsignificant.

records.title: Investigation of the correlation between changes in sweat chloride concentration and clinical improvement following initiation of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor
records.summary: Objectives: It is well-established that higher Sweat Chloride (SwCl) concentrations are associated with more severe cystic fibrosis (CF) phenotypes, and SwCl has been used as a surrogate marker of CFTR function restoration in clinical trials. After the introduction of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI), clinical outcomes have improved dramatically. In this study we aimed to examine the association between SwCl concentrations and improvements in clinical outcomes during ETI treatment. Methods: We conducted a nationwide observational study using the Danish CF Cohort, including individuals aged ≥6 years with at least one F508del mutation who have initiated ETI. Pre-ETI baseline was defined as the 12 months pr...
records.why_it_matters: Sweat chloride categories may not map neatly to ETI clinical benefit, limiting biomarker interpretation.
records.confidence_reason: Nationwide cohort with adjusted analyses across age strata and multiple outcomes.

records.title: Characterization of proton pump inhibitors (PPI) use in people with CF: real-world data from the European CF society patient registry
records.summary: Background: Proton pump inhibitors (PPI) are widely used in people with CF (pwCF); while improving airway surface liquid (ASL) pH, they may increase susceptibility to bacterial infections. We assessed factors associated with their use, as well as association with FEV1 and infection with Pseudomonas aeruginosa (PsA). Methods: European Cystic Fibrosis Society Patient Registry (ECFSPR) data (2019-2024) were used. PwCF were grouped by longitudinal PPI exposure: never used (reference), use from 2020 with 3–5 consecutive years of treatment (cohort 1), and persistent use across all years (cohort 2). Annual ppFEV1 and PsA over time were analyzed with mixed-effect regression models. Multiple logistic regression assesse...
records.why_it_matters: Widespread PPI use may mark or contribute to infection and lung-function differences, warranting treatment review.
records.confidence_reason: Large European registry analysis reports exposure groups and adjusted outcome patterns.

records.title: Comparative evaluation of cardiometabolic risk-factors in cystic fibrosis compared to other disease: a retrospective cohort study
records.summary: Background and Objectives: An aging cystic fibrosis (CF) population means that diseases of aging, including cardiovascular disease (CVD), are emerging as new concerns and may become more prevalent in people with CF (PwCF). PwCF are at an increased risk of adverse cardiovascular (CV) events, such as heart attack, stroke, and diabetes. However, CVD risk profiles remain poorly described in this group. Methods: A retrospective observation cohort study was performed utilising TriNetX global health network to determine CV risk profiles in adults with CF and observe 1-year CV outcomes (myocardial infarction, atrial fibrillation, heart failure, and a composite endpoint). Cardiometabolic disease codes were explored and...
records.why_it_matters: Ageing with CF brings cardiovascular risk into routine adult CF management.
records.confidence_reason: Retrospective cohort objective is clear, but visible results are incomplete.

records.title: Understanding cystic fibrosis-associated colorectal polyp and cancer development: bridging clinical insights and molecular mechanisms.
records.summary: Objectives: People with Cystic Fibrosis (pwCF) are living longer, primarily due to the introduction and optimization of CFTR modulator therapy. However, ageing in pwCF is associated with long-term complications, such as colorectal cancer (CRC) and polyps. PwCF aged 40-49 show a CRC prevalence of ~23%, compared to 2-5% in age-matched non-CF individuals. Thus, pwCF undergo surveillance colonoscopies for early detection of colorectal polyps and cancer. This study aims to improve understanding of CF-related CRC by combining epidemiological data with molecular analysis of corresponding patient-derived polyp tissue. Methods: Clinical data from pwCF who underwent surveillance colonoscopies was extracted from patient...
secondary_themes: Precision CFTR biology and rare variants
records.why_it_matters: Linking colonoscopy findings to polyp molecular features may clarify why CRC risk is elevated in CF.
records.confidence_reason: Rationale and translational approach are clear, but detailed results are not visible.

records.title: Audiological profiling in an era of CFTR modulation, including extended high frequency hearing assessment in a cohort of adult people with Cystic Fibrosis (pwCF). Preliminary results of 104 screened
records.summary: Introduction: pwCF are at increased risk of sensorineural hearing loss due to cumulative exposure to ototoxic therapies and CF-related comorbidities. Methods: A total of 104 adult pwCF were recruited. Baseline demographic data were collected, followed by comprehensive audiological assessment, including extended high-frequency pure-tone audiometry (250 Hz–20 kHz). Associations between covariates and hearing severity across all frequencies were analysed using multinomial ordinal regression with a repeated-measures random effect. Results: Age was significantly associated with hearing loss severity; for each additional year of age, participants were 19% more likely to be classified in a worse hearing threshold cat...
secondary_themes: Care burden, adherence, and psychosocial impact
records.why_it_matters: Audiology screening may need updating as adults live longer with cumulative ototoxic exposures.
records.confidence_reason: Preliminary cohort size and methods are clear, with partial early results.

records.title: Parenthood impact on body mass index among people with cystic fibrosis: findings from the HOPeCF retrospective cohort study
records.summary: Objective: We examined body mass index (BMI) between parents and nonparents with CF (PwCF; NPwCF) using retrospective data from the Health Outcomes of Parents with CF (HOPeCF) study. Methods: We surveyed PwCF who became new parents between 2012-2022 and NPwCF and linked results to 2010-2023 patient registry data. We created 2012-2018 and 2019-2022 cohorts and designated time points as year -1 and -2 (pre-parenthood), year 0 (parenthood onset or 2015 and 2021 for NPwCF), and year 1-5 (post-parenthood). We evaluated longitudinal BMI change using multivariable longitudinal linear regression models adjusted for age, race, lung function, ever ivacaftor or elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor(ETI) use, insurance status,...
secondary_themes: Care burden, adherence, and psychosocial impact
records.why_it_matters: BMI trajectories around parenthood inform nutritional support for adults balancing CF and caregiving.
records.confidence_reason: Linked cohort design is clear, but visible results are incomplete.

records.title: Newborn screening and early outcomes in cystic fibrosis: ECFS Patient Registry Data
records.summary: Objectives: Newborn screening (NBS) for cystic fibrosis (CF) enables early diagnosis and prompt initiation of treatment. This study compares clinical outcomes in children with CF diagnosed through NBS with those diagnosed clinically. Methods: Data were obtained from the European Cystic Fibrosis Society Patient Registry (ECFSPR), which in 2023 included over 56,000 people with CF from 42 countries. To account for temporal changes in care and outcomes, screen-detected (SD) and clinically diagnosed (CD) patients were compared across three five-year birth cohorts: 2008–2012 (SD n=4,118; CD n=3,755), 2013–2017 (SD n=5,292; CD n=2,803), and 2018–2022 (SD n=5,282; CD n=1,181). Outcomes included lung function (ppFEV1),...
records.why_it_matters: Newborn screening outcome comparisons quantify the clinical value of early diagnosis across Europe.
records.confidence_reason: Large ECFSPR analysis with birth cohorts and defined pediatric outcomes.

records.title: An evaluation of Cystic Fibrosis Screen Positive Inconclusive Diagnosis (CFSPID) patients across the Alder Hey Children's Hospital shared-care network since the implementation of new-born screening
records.summary: Introduction: Alder Hey Children’s Hospital (AHCH), UK, receives several notifications from the new-born screening team every year. Most of the children identified are diagnosed with cystic fibrosis (CF) and follow clear care pathways. Fewer children are diagnosed with cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator-related metabolic syndrome/cystic fibrosis screen positive, inconclusive diagnosis (CRMS/CFSPID). In 2021 Barben, Jürg et al published ‘Updated guidance on the management of children CRMS/CFSPID’ in the Journal of Cystic Fibrosis, to help standardise care in this smaller population. Objectives: To identify all children diagnosed with CFSPID across the network since the introduction of new-born...
secondary_themes: Service delivery, access, and multidisciplinary care
records.why_it_matters: CFSPID pathway review supports consistent follow-up for infants with inconclusive newborn screening results.
records.confidence_reason: Network evaluation objective is clear, but visible outcome detail is partial.

records.title: Temporal and spatial trends in cystic fibrosis incidence over 35 years of newborn screening in Brittany (western France)
records.summary: Objectives: Brittany (western France) is known to have one of the highest incidences of cystic fibrosis (CF) worldwide. The very early implementation of newborn screening (NBS) for CF in this region - 35 years ago - offers the opportunity to accurately monitor long-term trends in incidence and detection of initial potential changes due to the advent of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI). The aim of this study was to explore temporal and spatial trends in the incidence of CF in Brittany since NBS for CF was set up. Methods: This study included all newborns with CF detected in Brittany since the implementation of NBS in 1989 (n=358). Temporal and spatial changes in incidence rates were modelled using Poisson...
records.why_it_matters: Long-running Brittany newborn screening data can detect temporal and geographic shifts in CF incidence.
records.confidence_reason: Thirty-five-year screened cohort and incidence modelling are described.

records.title: Benchmarking Dutch cystic fibrosis care: integrating centre metrics with patient‑reported experience measures to inform national guidelines
records.summary: Objectives: Variation in the organization and delivery of cystic fibrosis (CF) care may affect patient experience and clinical outcomes. Previously, we demonstrated that patient-driven quality of care improvement is possible (Noordhoek, JCF, 2023). We aimed to benchmark Dutch CF centres in the CFTR-modulator era, using patient‑reported experience measures (PREMs), and inform revision of the Dutch CF standard‑of‑care guidelines. Methods: All Dutch CF centres (7 adult, 7 paediatric) completed a survey in 2025 on team composition, diagnostics, treatments, and organization. Parallel online questionnaires collected PREMs and overall care ratings from people with CF (pwCF) and parents of children with CF. Analyses w...
records.primary_theme: Service delivery, access, and multidisciplinary care
secondary_themes: Care burden, adherence, and psychosocial impact
records.why_it_matters: Benchmarking centre structures with PREMs can align Dutch guidelines with patient experience in the modulator era.
records.confidence_reason: National centre survey and parallel patient-experience methods are specified.

records.title: A low-cost, standardized cystic fibrosis registry architecture for low- and middle-income countries: the International Cystic Fibrosis Training Network (ICFTN) experience
records.summary: Background: Patient registries are key to improving cystic fibrosis (CF) care and enabling outcomes research. Many low- and middle-income countries (LMICs) lack access to national CF registries due to financial, infrastructure, and technical constraints, resulting in underrepresentation of these populations in global CF data. Methods: We aimed to develop a CF registry on the REDCap platform, designed for low-cost deployment and rapid implementation. The registry was activated in June 2022 and currently includes 11 participating centers from two countries, with 554 unique patients and 72 active clinical users. To ensure international standardization, the registry integrated Global Lung Function Initiative (GLI)...
records.primary_theme: Service delivery, access, and multidisciplinary care
records.why_it_matters: A low-cost REDCap registry model can broaden CF data capture in low- and middle-income countries.
records.confidence_reason: Implementation details and scale are given, but outcomes are mainly programmatic.

records.title: Evaluation of professional quality of life and prevalence of burnout: A national survey of healthcare providers on cystic fibrosis (CF) care teams
records.summary: Objectives: CF team-based care may reduce burnout. We examined moderators of the relationship between Burnout and Professional Satisfaction. Methods: CF clinicians completed Maslach Burnout Inventory (MBI), Professional Quality of Life Scale (ProQOL). Principal Factoring Analysis (PFA) with promax rotation identified which subscales formed composite factors. PFA and general linear models evaluated moderation. Results: N=569: 24.9% physicians, 21.9% nurses/advanced practitioners, 29.4% allied health, 23.8% mental health providers (MH); 48.3% pediatric, 47.8% in CF >10 years. PFA yielded 2 factors: 1)“Burnout:” MBI Depersonalization (0.72), MBI Emotional Exhaustion (0.94), ProQOL Burnout (0.90), ProQOL Secondary...
records.primary_theme: Care burden, adherence, and psychosocial impact
secondary_themes: Service delivery, access, and multidisciplinary care
records.why_it_matters: Provider burnout measurement identifies workforce risks that can affect multidisciplinary CF care.
records.confidence_reason: National survey reports sample size, provider mix, and validated burnout instruments.

records.title: PESQI: Peer-led Evaluation for Shared learning and Quality Improvement in cystic fibrosis
records.summary: Objectives: Clinical peer review is a formal process whereby health professionals evaluate a service or colleague’s practice to improve care and facilitate shared learning. In a resource restricted health system, we sought to develop a pragmatic, cost-neutral approach to peer-led review of multidisciplinary cystic fibrosis (CF) care in the United Kingdom (UK). Methods: An initial online consultation was carried out with CF professionals to gather views on value and preferred format of peer review. A dedicated Executive of CF professionals and community representatives was set up to co-create an initial model, which was then refined with input from the CF Medical Association and Cystic Fibrosis Trust Clinical A...
records.primary_theme: Service delivery, access, and multidisciplinary care
records.why_it_matters: A pragmatic peer-review model may support quality improvement when service resources are constrained.
records.confidence_reason: Co-design process is clear, but implementation outcomes are not fully visible.

records.title: The Canadian cystic fibrosis consensus guidelines: integrating the role of the multi-disciplinary team
records.summary: Objective: To describe the integration of the multi-disciplinary team roles into the Canadian cystic fibrosis (CF) consensus guidelines Methods: In 2022, CF Canada began developing the Canadian CF consensus guidelines. Informed by updated standards from the European CF Society and CF Foundation, CF Canada sought to develop a holistic set of guidelines that integrated the role of the multi-disciplinary team, encompassed care across the lifespan for people with CF (pwCF) and reflected changes in practice since the introduction of modulator therapies; all from a uniquely Canadian perspective. A steering committee was formed that had representation from across Canada and all roles. Each member was responsible for...
records.primary_theme: Service delivery, access, and multidisciplinary care
records.why_it_matters: Canadian consensus guideline development embeds multidisciplinary roles for care across the lifespan.
records.confidence_reason: Process and rationale are clear, but the abstract is primarily descriptive.

records.title: A mixed-methods evaluation of the International Cystic Fibrosis Training Network program, a multidisciplinary training model to improve cystic fibrosis care in low- and middle-income countries
records.summary: Objective: To evaluate the potential influence of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) therapy on the progression of pancreatic imaging abnormalities in patients with Cystic Fibrosis. Methods: A total of 196 patients with Cystic Fibrosis treated with ETI and who underwent High-Resolution Computed Tomography (HRCT) were included (93 males, 103 females; mean age at first CT 15 years; mean age at start of therapy 24 years). A longitudinal evaluation of the pancreatic parenchyma included in the caudal portions of chest HRCT scans was performed using available pre- and post-therapy examinations (mean follow-up 88 months; 774 CT scans analyzed). Pancreatic parenchyma was independently segmented by two readers, and...
records.why_it_matters: Pancreatic imaging trajectories suggest ETI may alter, but not fully stop, structural pancreatic progression.
records.confidence_reason: Structured pancreatic imaging abstract is clear, but the title conflicts with the abstract topic.

records.title: Advancing cystic fibrosis care in Canada: developing a national quality improvement model
records.summary: Objectives: Rapid advances in cystic fibrosis (CF) therapy have reshaped clinical needs in Canada. In response, Cystic Fibrosis Canada collaborated with clinicians and community members (2023–25) to develop new Canadian Consensus Guidelines for CF Care. Evidence shows that guideline implementation declines without structured planning and monitoring. To address this, Cystic Fibrosis Canada is developing a national quality improvement (QI) program to support uptake, adherence, and identification of care gaps. Methods: An environmental scan and literature review were conducted to identify comparable QI models, indicators, and outcomes. Interviews will be conducted with clinicians to explore the role of QI and imp...
records.primary_theme: Service delivery, access, and multidisciplinary care
records.why_it_matters: A national QI model can improve guideline uptake and reveal centre-level care gaps.
records.confidence_reason: Framework and anticipated indicators are described, but clinician interviews are prospective.

records.title: The role of the paediatrician in managing patients with cystic fibrosis in the absence of specialised centers
records.summary: Objectives: In recent decades, advances in the diagnosis and treatment of cystic fibrosis (CF) have significantly increased the life expectancy and quality of life of people with CF (pwCF) in developed countries. However, in countries without specialised CF centers, there are a number of acute problems that make our study very important. Aim: To study the clinical features of of children with CF (cwCF) in our hospital and determine the role of the paediatrician in managing cwCF in the absence of a specialised center. Methods: Eight cwCF, from 1 to 6 years old (all diagnosed in the Russian Federation (RF)), were monitored by a paediatrician, of whom 6 were repeatedly hospitalized in the National Medical Center...
records.primary_theme: Service delivery, access, and multidisciplinary care
records.why_it_matters: Paediatricians may be essential to maintaining CF care where specialized centres are absent.
records.confidence_reason: Small pediatric programme context is clear, but evidence is limited.

records.title: Building and implementing a data core team to support a cystic fibrosis learning network.
records.summary: Objectives: The United States Cystic Fibrosis Learning Network (CFLN) is a maturing, collaborative, multisite, multi-stakeholder, data-driven network that aims to improve outcomes for all people with CF (PwCF) and their families. The structure of CFLN relies on timely and reliable data summaries that are transparently distributed to the network to enhance their quality improvement aims. The University of Virginia Data Core (UVADC) is a collaborative group of professionals with diverse backgrounds aiming to support quality improvement initiatives for the CFLN. Our goal is to build, implement, and adapt tools to support quality improvement projects through rigorous data capture systems and enhanced communication...
records.primary_theme: Service delivery, access, and multidisciplinary care
records.why_it_matters: A data core can make learning-network improvement work faster and more transparent.
records.confidence_reason: Programme goals and data-support role are clear, but outcomes are process-oriented.

records.title: Development of a universal Core Outcome Set for Cystic Fibrosis (COS-CF): eDelphi consensus process
records.summary: Objectives: There is a need for greater consensus on the outcomes used in cystic fibrosis (CF) clinical trials to enable results to be compared and synthesised across trials. The development of a universal Core Outcome Set for CF (COS-CF) aims to address this need by defining outcomes that should be measured in all late-phase interventional trials in CF, regardless of their specific focus. Methods: Our protocol is registered on Figshare (DOI: 10.6084/m9.figshare.28044917) and the COMET initiative database. Three rounds of eDelphi survey are being conducted to gain consensus among stakeholders about the most important outcomes to measure in CF trials. In rounds 1 and 2, participants rate the importance of outco...
records.primary_theme: Service delivery, access, and multidisciplinary care
records.why_it_matters: A core outcome set can reduce heterogeneity across CF trials and improve evidence synthesis.
records.confidence_reason: Registered eDelphi process, stakeholder groups, and interim consensus results are reported.

records.title: Measuring what matters: physiotherapy outcomes in cystic fibrosis care
records.summary: Background: Cystic fibrosis (CF) registries underpin population surveillance, service planning and evaluation of therapeutic outcomes. Registry datasets infrequently capture functional or physiotherapy-related outcomes central to contemporary care. This study considered the heterogeneity of physiotherapy outcome-measures (OMs) and the value of clinically relevant OMs for possible inclusion in expanding registry datasets. Methods: An online survey was distributed to physiotherapists working in CF across UK, Europe, Australia, New Zealand, USA and Canada. Quantitative data summarised the frequency and consistency of OM use, while qualitative data explored the contextual and organisational factors shaping OM deci...
records.why_it_matters: Adding physiotherapy outcomes to registries would better capture functional health in modern CF care.
records.confidence_reason: International physiotherapist survey reports sample size and convergence in outcome use.

records.title: Comparison of hospitalisation rates in screen detected & clinically diagnosed children with cystic fibrosis in first five years:Findings from Irish Comparative Outcomes Study of Cystic Fibrosis(ICOS)
records.summary: Objectives: Newborn screening for cystic fibrosis (CF) was introduced in Ireland in 2011. The ICOS national cohort study compares children with CF (CWCF) diagnosed through newborn screening with those diagnosed clinically. We examined risk factors for hospitalisation due to pulmonary exacerbations and other CF-related conditions in first five years. Methods: Study includes CWCF born between July 2008 - June 2023. Hospitalisation data were collected on both screen-detected and clinically diagnosed cohorts from birth to 60 months from CF Registry, Ireland and hospital charts. Logistic regression was used to identify demographic and clinical factors associated with hospitalisation in first 60 months of life in CW...
records.why_it_matters: Irish newborn-screening comparisons clarify hospitalization risk in early childhood CF.
records.confidence_reason: National cohort with registry and chart-linked hospitalization data is described.

records.title: Updating the Cystic Fibrosis Questionnaire-Revised (CFQ-R) in the era of highly effective modulators: A qualitative and crosswalk study to develop the CFQ-M
records.summary: Objectives: Developed in 2000, the Cystic Fibrosis Questionnaire-Revised (CFQ-R) has been used extensively in clinical and behavioral studies and considered the “gold standard” health-related quality of life (HRQoL) measure for people with cystic fibrosis (PWCF) ages 2+ years. With the advent of highly effective modulators (ETI, VTD), substantial improvements in lung function, body mass index, and HRQoL have been documented. Thus, the lived experience of PWCF has changed dramatically, requiring an expansion of items and scales on the CFQ-R to reflect this clinical progress. This study aims to conduct qualitative research with PWCF to elicit concepts for a modulator-specific version of the CFQ-R (CFQ-M) and eva...
records.primary_theme: Care burden, adherence, and psychosocial impact
records.why_it_matters: Updating CFQ-R for modulator-era experience may keep quality-of-life measurement clinically meaningful.
records.confidence_reason: Multi-phase qualitative and psychometric plan is clearly specified.

records.title: Mapping current practice & preferences in the use of PROMs in the clinical setting in Ireland and an international review of the inclusion of PROMs in Cystic Fibrosis patient registries
records.summary: Objectives: The use of patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs), including symptom scores, wellbeing measures, and Health-Related Quality of Life measures, are of increasing interest to help measure the burden of CF. While there has been recent interest in the use of PROMs as outcome measures in clinical trials, there is limited long-term real-world data published by registries. Longitudinal PROMs data can track change, help inform clinical practice and empower patients to better understand and manage their condition. Patient registries play an important role to support these goals given their longitudinal nature and rich clinical outcome datasets. To explore the feasibility of the inclusion of PROMs within a...
records.primary_theme: Care burden, adherence, and psychosocial impact
secondary_themes: Service delivery, access, and multidisciplinary care
records.why_it_matters: PROM registry integration could make longitudinal patient burden visible in routine CF data.
records.confidence_reason: Survey and international registry review methods are explicit.

records.title: Early outcomes of a Twinning partnership between Istanbul-Cerrahpasa and King's College London: a SWOT perspective
records.summary: Background: The Twinning Expansion Project aims to develop a long-term centre-to-centre partnership in which an established cystic fibrosis (CF) centre guides and supports another site to optimise care for people with CF. Istanbul-Cerrahpasa and King’s College London were matched as mentii and mentor in 2024 spring. Objective: To assess the early phase of this partnership using SWOT analysis. Methods: Some team members first met in person during the ECFS Conference in Glasgow. An online meeting followed in July 2024 to introduce both teams and plan the process. Cerrahpasa presented the organisation of the centre and patient characteristics. To date, four cross-site clinical and teaching meetings have taken pla...
records.primary_theme: Service delivery, access, and multidisciplinary care
records.why_it_matters: Twinning partnerships can transfer practical CF expertise and support centre development.
records.confidence_reason: SWOT-based early programme evaluation is useful but not outcome-heavy.

records.title: Experience of the ECFS-Twinning project between Cluj-Napoca and Brussels
records.summary: Background: The goal of ECFS-Twinning project is to set up a site-to-site partnership, aiming to fill the gap between European countries regarding care and clinical outcomes in people with CF. Twinning combines mentorship-led knowledge transfer with practical implementation to the selected mentee site. In 2020, collaboration between the CF centre of the Pediatrics Clinic 1 Cluj, Romania, (mentee) and UZ Brussel, Belgium, (mentor) started as part of this project. Methods: First online meetings were exploring and showed an important language barrier, complicating real conversations. Consequently, Dr Szabo followed English course. It took a year before regular virtual meetings could take place. The first on-site...
records.primary_theme: Service delivery, access, and multidisciplinary care
records.why_it_matters: Twinning experience identifies language, staffing, and physiotherapy-practice barriers to equitable CF care.
records.confidence_reason: Programme history and qualitative outcomes are described, but evidence is descriptive.

records.title: How to deliver digital health literacy training to PwCF, families, and caregivers: results of an Irish survey
records.summary: Background: Digital Health Literacy (DHL) combines health literacy and digital skills to safely and confidently use digital health tools. CF Digital Health Tools (DHT) such as telehealth, AI searches, remote monitoring, and online forums are increasingly common in CF care, creating a need to build DHL knowledge. Aim & objective: To identify effective modes for delivering DHL training and resources to PwCF and their caregivers in Ireland. Methods: A Public Patient Involvement (PPI) team of 8 members (5 PwCF, 3 parents of PwCF) developed a survey for the Irish CF community to explore their understanding of DHL and how best to deliver this training and resources. This survey was digitised and made available onlin...
records.primary_theme: Service delivery, access, and multidisciplinary care
secondary_themes: Care burden, adherence, and psychosocial impact
records.why_it_matters: Digital health literacy support is needed so telehealth and online tools do not widen capability gaps.
records.confidence_reason: PPI-led survey reports respondents, knowledge gaps, and preferred formats.

records.title: Cloud-based remote care for cystic fibrosis: feasibility and patient outcomes
records.summary: Objective: Digital Home Hospital (DHH) solutions can transform cystic fibrosis (CF) care by enabling remote monitoring, reducing clinic visits, and providing personalised support. At Oslo University Hospital (OUH), the Dignio platform, a cloud-based mobile system, was introduced to track patient-reported data and clinical metrics. This study evaluated treatment adherence, patient satisfaction, the accuracy of home spirometry (HS) versus stationary spirometry (SS), and whether lung function remained stable during DHH. Methods: Of 61 adults with CF (≥17 years) in the Norwegian CF Registry with forced expiratory volume in one second (FEV₁%) >40 and receiving full care at OUH, 14 participants were randomly selecte...
records.primary_theme: Service delivery, access, and multidisciplinary care
records.why_it_matters: Remote monitoring showed feasibility, satisfaction, reliable home spirometry, and stable lung function.
records.confidence_reason: One-year digital follow-up reports adherence, satisfaction, spirometry agreement, and clinical stability.

records.title: Mapping current practices of digital health in respiratory disease management: national survey
records.summary: Background: Chronic respiratory diseases are long-term, irreversible disorders associated with substantial treatment burden and ongoing care needs. Digital health technologies are increasingly promoted to support self-management, monitoring, and care delivery in long-term conditions. However, evidence on their actual use, implementation, and perceived impact within respiratory care remains limited. Objectives: This study aimed to (1) describe current use (or non-use) of digital health technologies in the management of respiratory diseases among patients and healthcare professionals, (2) identify reported barriers, enablers, and potential inequalities influencing its use and implementation within NHS settings,...
records.primary_theme: Service delivery, access, and multidisciplinary care
secondary_themes: Care burden, adherence, and psychosocial impact
records.why_it_matters: Respiratory digital-health adoption varies, so implementation needs attention to barriers and inequalities.

records.title: Feasibility and acceptability of a digital, cognitive behavioural therapy-based group intervention for adolescents with chronic physical illness and their parents: a Swedish adaptation of On Track
records.summary: Objectives: digital treatment formats are essential to improve equitable access to healthcare and reduce treatment burden. Around 20% of children in western countries live with chronic physical illness (CPI), such as cystic fibrosis. CPI limits daily functioning and entails psychosocial challenges, across different CPI, including anxiety and depression. Parents play a key role in adolescents’ well-being and coping with illness-related stress, underscoring the need for interventions involving both adolescents and parents. To address this, the psychosocial support program On Track (OT), a digital, transdiagnostic group intervention based on Cognitive Behavioural Therapy, was developed in the Netherlands, to prov...
records.primary_theme: Care burden, adherence, and psychosocial impact
secondary_themes: Service delivery, access, and multidisciplinary care
records.why_it_matters: Digital CBT group support may improve coping and family communication for adolescents with chronic illness.
records.confidence_reason: Qualitative findings are positive but based on very few completed interviews.

records.title: Virtual clinical reviews in paediatric CF care - development of a service based on patient experience and clinical parameters
records.summary: Introduction: Advances in CF care have resulted in lower morbidity in children with CF (CwCF). Telehealth has the potential to allow clinical monitoring of CwCF with less disruption to daily life. Adoption of telehealth in CwCF has been slow, due to factors such as unreliability of equipment in younger children, reliance on parents to recognise subtle changes in their child’s health, safeguarding concerns & parental wishes. Within our centre we aimed to ascertain parental views on the development of a virtual clinic (VC) and develop a clinical checklist to stratify patient suitability for a VC appointment based on their annual assessment (AA) results. Methods: A PREM was sent to all families within the CF cent...
records.primary_theme: Service delivery, access, and multidisciplinary care
records.why_it_matters: Virtual paediatric reviews can reduce disruption if clinical checklists protect safety.
records.confidence_reason: Family PREM response rate, concerns, benefits, and checklist development are reported.

records.title: Clinical utility of point-of-care sweat chloride testing across contemporary cystic fibrosis care scenarios: a provider survey
records.summary: Objectives: Sweat chloride is a validated biomarker of CFTR function and response to CFTR modulators, yet its use beyond diagnosis is limited by logistical barriers. Emerging point-of-care (POC) technologies may enable rapid assessment of CFTR modulation in routine care. We evaluated clinician perceptions of the realism and clinical utility of POC sweat chloride testing across contemporary cystic fibrosis (CF) care scenarios. Methods: We conducted a cross-sectional survey of CF clinicians evaluating seven scenarios in which rapid (10–15 minute) POC sweat chloride results could inform care, including CFTR modulator adherence, drug–drug interactions, atypical modulator response, exercise-related hydration manage...
records.why_it_matters: Point-of-care sweat chloride could make individualized modulator monitoring more actionable.
records.confidence_reason: Clinician survey provides scenario ratings across multiple contemporary care uses.

records.title: Maintaining skills, evolving roles, enhancing knowledge: physiotherapy in cystic fibrosis
records.summary: Introduction: With highly effective modulators and specialised multidisciplinary care reshaping cystic fibrosis (CF) management, physiotherapy roles continue to evolve. While many individuals experience reduced symptom burden, others face complex exacerbations, or limited access to modulators, and increased complexity with ageing. To ensure ongoing access to evidence-based resources, Australian physiotherapists (with ongoing input from global clinicians) established CFPhysio.com Inc (CFP Inc), a not-for-profit organisation supporting professional development (PD) through digital technology. Method: Specialist physiotherapists translated evidence-based guidelines into an online learning platform offering free,...
secondary_themes: Service delivery, access, and multidisciplinary care
records.why_it_matters: Online physiotherapy education helps clinicians maintain CF-specific skills as roles evolve.
records.confidence_reason: Platform analytics and registration survey data are useful but programmatic.

records.title: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)-based computational modelling of particle deposition in the lungs of people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF)
records.summary: Objectives: Optimising the delivery of inhaled therapies for CF requires an understanding of the impact of structural and functional effects of CF lung disease on particle deposition. These are not adequately represented in current computational deposition models. Our aim is to develop a personalised lung deposition model for pwCF based on 1H and 129Xe-MRI. Methods: In one volunteer with CF (M, age = 44.8 yrs, ppFEV1 = 64.9), ultrashort echo time (UTE) 1H-MRI was used to segment visible airways and then further generate an idealised model of airway structure. From their 129Xe ventilation MRI, ventilation defects were linked to the corresponding airways in the model. These airways were then constricted to achie...
secondary_themes: Emerging genetic, RNA, and cell-model therapies
records.why_it_matters: Patient-specific MRI modelling may improve prediction of inhaled drug deposition in structurally abnormal CF lungs.
records.confidence_reason: Detailed modelling workflow is provided, but it is based on one volunteer.

records.title: Evolution of exercise capacity in adults with cystic fibrosis receiving ETI
records.summary: Background:Prior to the introduction of CFTR modulation CF lung disease was characterised by progressive decline in lung functionCardio-pulmonary exercise testing (CPET) has been reported to provide additional prognostic information beyond lung function measurements. The utilisation of Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (ETI) has resulted in improvements in lung function measurements in adults with CF but less is known on its sustained impact on exercise capacity.Aim:Cystic fibrosis (CF) is associated with progressive decline in lung function and reduced exercise tolerance. records.title: Highly neutralizing antibodies to multidrug-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa associate with colonization status, immunological alterations and lung disease progression in cystic fibrosis
records.summary: Objectives: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa) airway colonization in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) requires close monitoring due to its association with increased pulmonary exacerbations, progressive lung damage and accelerated decline in pulmonary function. Prior studies from our group identified pwCF who produced highly neutralizing antibodies against the type III secretion system (T3SS) of Pa. The aim of this work is to investigate the clinical relevance of these antibodies and immunological mechanisms driving chronic Pa infection. Methods: We quantified serum T3SS-neutralizing activity in 99 pwCF and 12 healthy individuals. Neutralization capacity was integrated with immunophenotyping, including FACS-based...

records.why_it_matters: Anti-Pseudomonas immune signatures may help flag chronic infection and lung-function decline risk.

records.title: Restoration of CFTR function by vanzacaftor/tezacaftor/deutivacaftor in primary nasal epithelial cells from people with cystic fibrosis carrying the F508del-L467F complex allele
records.summary: Background: Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) improves F508del-CFTR function and leads to substantial clinical benefits. However, people with CF and the F508del mutation and the complex allele L467F showed limited clinical response to ETI. Recently, vanzacaftor/tezacaftor/deutivacaftor (VTD) was approved as an alternative modulator therapy for people with CF and at least one non-class I mutation. However, the effects of VTD in patients carrying the complex allele L467F have not been investigated. Within an ECFS Twinning project in collaboration with the CF center in Skopje, North Macedonia, we therefore assessed the pharmacological rescue of CFTR activity by ETI and VTD in primary nasal epithelial cells (...

records.title: A decade of change: employment, education and training outcomes for 16-24 year olds in CF (2015 – 2025)
records.summary: Objectives: The past decade has seen growth in the number of young people with CF (YPwCF) in the All Wales Adult CF Centre, as well as the introduction of CFTR modulators earlier in life. Given these changes, we compared YPwCF’s involvement in education, employment and training in 2025 with 2015. Method: Employment and education data collected at annual review in 2015 (n=71, 44 M, 27 F, mean age 21, mean FEV1 76.7%) were compared to 2025 data (n=91, 48 M, 39 F, 4 TG, mean age 20, mean FEV1 93.4%) for patients in the adult service aged 16-24. records.title: Fluorescent organoid-based screening models for cystic fibrosis drug discovery
records.summary: Objectives: CFTR modulators have transformed cystic fibrosis (CF) care; however, variability in patient responses highlights the need for more predictive and physiologically relevant functional assays. Patient-derived intestinal organoids (PDIOs) enable high-throughput, yet indirect assessment of CFTR activity through the forskolin-induced swelling (FIS) assay.1 Here, we aim to establish fluorescence-based assays for direct, quantitative, real-time readouts of CFTR activity in PDIO-derived systems using genetically encoded ion-sensitive fluorescent sensors, such as HS-YFP-mKate2 for iodide flux and ClopHensor3 for chloride and pH measurements. Methods: HS-YFP-mKate and Clop3 constructs were cloned into lentivi...

records.title: Association between diet, diet quality, and gastrointestinal symptom phenotypes in adults with cystic fibrosis
records.summary: Objectives: People with cystic fibrosis (CF) have historically been advised to eat an unrestricted high-calorie, high-fat “legacy CF diet”, which is associated with poor diet quality, and may exacerbate gastrointestinal symptoms (GiS). This study examined associations between diet, dietary patterns and GiS. Method: Adults from three UK CF centres completed GiS questionnaires to derive clusters using Latent Class Analysis. This provided four distinct phenotypes mild, moderate-diarrhoea, moderate-constipation and severe. Participants completed at least one 24-hour dietary recall using Intake24. Following quality checks, mean intake across two days was calculated. Diet quality and inflammatory potential were asse...

records.title: Effect of the CFTR modulators on liver status; analysis of the Irish Longitudinal Study of Liver Disease in cystic fibrosis
records.summary: Objectives: Cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) modulators have beneficial effects on the respiratory and nutritional status of people with Cystic Fibrosis (CF) but the impact on liver involvement is not clear. The objective was to use a prospective study of children with CF to compare liver status over time in those taking modulators versus those who were not. Methods: A national longitudinal study collects data on children with CF every five years, with a focus on liver parameters. Children treated with any CFTR modulator who had liver status recorded at T0 (pre-modulator) and T1 (post-modulator) were designated cases and compared to controls not treated with modulator. records.title: Carbapenem-resistance oprD mutations reshape Pseudomonas aeruginosa host-pathogen interactions during infection
records.summary: Objectives: Antibiotic-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa poses a major challenge in chronic airway disease, particularly in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). Carbapenem-resistant P. aeruginosa is considered a highest-priority bacterial threat, with resistance frequently driven by loss-of-function mutations in the carbapenem uptake porin OprD. However, the broader consequences of these mutations for bacterial physiology and infection dynamics remain poorly understood. Methods: Clinical P. aeruginosa strains isolated longitudinally from pwCF were analyzed to assess the emergence of oprD mutations. records.title: Personalized medicine for cystic fibrosis (CF) rare genotypes: uncovering a misclassified class I mutation and guiding successful treatment in a person with CF carrying the G542X/622-2A>C genotype
records.summary: Background: People with Cystic fibrosis carrying two class I CFTR mutations are generally considered non-responsive to CFTR modulators. According to recent literature [1], the genotype G542X/622-2A>C is predicted as Class I/I, that would result in no protein production, precluding benefit from CFTR modulators. However, 622-2A>C is a very rare CFTR undercharacterized variant. Methods: Patient-derived rectal organoids (PDROs) from a woman with with CF carrying the G542X/622-2A>C genotype were analyzed via RT-PCR/sequencing of complete CFTR cDNA, immunoblot, and forskolin-induced swelling (FIS) assays with ivacaftor(I), tezacaftor/I (TI), and elexacaftor/TI (ETI). records.title: Caregiver burden and quality of life of parents of children with cystic fibrosis in the Irish Comparative Outcomes Study of CF (ICOS); the CarerQol Questionnaire
records.summary: There is a relative paucity of research examining the impact of informal caregiving on parents of young children with cystic fibrosis (CF). The aim of this study was to examine caregiver burden and identify risk factors associated with high caregiver burden in parents of young children with CF using the CarerQol questionnaire. This was a cross-sectional study. The CarerQoL questionnaire is a validated tool composed of two parts: (i) the CarerQol-7D which describes the care situation in terms of the negative and positive impact of caregiving and (ii) the visual analogue scale (VAS) which measures happiness on a scale from 0 (unhappy) -10 (happy). records.title: Are people with cystic fibrosis, treated with modulator medicines ingesting too much salt, putting them at increased risk of hypertension? A retrospective population cohort study
records.summary: Background: Historically, people with cystic fibrosis (CF) have been advised to consume a high-sodium diet to compensate for excessive sweat sodium losses caused by CFTR dysfunction. Highly effective CFTR modulator therapy improves CFTR function and reduces sweat sodium losses, potentially altering sodium balance. Persistently high sodium intake increases risk of hypertension, an emerging concern as the CF population ages. Objective: To investigate whether adults with CF receiving CFTR modulator therapy exhibit high urinary sodium concentrations [uNa] as a proxy for high dietary sodium intake. Method: Retrospective cohort study using routinely collected spot urinary sodium and sodium/creatinine concentrations....

records.title: Longitudinal trypsin trajectories as a biomarker of pancreatic disease severity in cystic fibrosis
records.summary: Background: Pancreas disease is one of the first disease complication in Cystic Fibrosis (CF) reflected by high immunoreactive trypsin (IRT) at birth used as CF screening test. Although a postnatal decline in serum trypsin has previously been described, longitudinal trypsin trajectories across childhood have not been well characterized. We leveraged the longitudinal clinical data and serum trypsin measurements in the Toronto CF Database (TCF) to estimate trypsin trajectories and their association with clinical outcomes. records.summary: Introduction: Patient-specific three-dimensional (3D) nasal epithelial cell cultures have strong potential to improve diagnostic accuracy and support biomarker development and functional assays in translational research. These models bridge bench and bedside and enable functional investigation of respiratory diseases such as cystic fibrosis and primary ciliary dyskinesia through assessment of CFTR activity and ciliary function. records.title: Impact of modulators on colonising PA in patients with cystic fibrosis: phenotypic and genomic changes during adaptation
records.summary: Objectives: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (PA) causes chronic lung infections in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). Small molecules known as modulators partially restore the defective protein causing CF. Despite several patients experience sputum load decreases and apparently resolve chronic bacterial infection, PA may persist through adaptation. We investigated how PA adapts to the changed environment in pwCF during modulators therapy by integrating phenotypic and genomic data. Methods: We investigated 563 PA strains isolated from 35 chronically colonised pwCF collected from a maximum of 15 years pre-treatment and up to 3 years post-treatment with elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor (ETI) or previous modulators. records.title: Allele-specific editing in primary human nasal epithelial cells with compound heterozygous genotypes
records.summary: With over 650 identified disease-causing CFTR variants, approximately 51% of people with Cystic Fibrosis carry a compound heterozygous (CH) genotype; among these, 82% harbour at least one F508del allele – representing around 68,000 people. Hence, it is central to therapeutic advancement to develop strategies that target compound heterozygotes, as correcting or functionally rescuing even one of their pathogenic alleles could shift an individual towards an improved phenotype. As complex genotypes may necessitate distinct combinations of modulators or gene-editing approaches, a broader strategy that can be tailored to address the specific variants would expand therapeutic possibilities. records.title: Physical, mental health, and neurocognitive side-effects in adolescents and adults with cystic fibrosis taking elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor: A 24-month multidimensional assessment
records.summary: Objectives: Although clinical studies have reported positive effects of ETI in people with cystic fibrosis (PWCF), longitudinal data are limited. These results are part of an ongoing longitudinal study on the global effects of ETI in adolescents and adults. Methods: PWCF aged ≥12 years eligible for ETI completed longitudinal assessments at baseline (T0), 1-mos (T1), 3-mos (T2), 6-mos (T3), 12-mos (T4), 18-mos (T5), 24-mos (T6) after ETI. Measures: a) physical parameters (FEV₁, BMI, GI Symptom Tracker); b) HRQoL (CFQ-R); c) depression (PHQ-9) & anxiety (GAD-7); d) cognitive processing (SDMT); e) ad hoc neurocognitive side-effects questionnaire. records.title: Salt intake in cystic fibrosis under highly effective modulator therapy (HEMT): toward an individualized approach
records.summary: Optimizing sodium intake in patients with CF (pwCF) under high effective modulator therapy (HEMT) remains challenging. While some suggest requirements match those of the general population. We explored an individualized approach to salt management. Methods: We performed a retrospective, single-center analysis at the adult and pediatric Montpellier CF Center. Salt requirements were assessed under standardized conditions by comparing patients’ salt intake with French High Authority for Health recommendations and urinary Na/K ratio (A urinary Na/K ratio < 1 may indicate insufficient sodium intake, particularly under high salt loss conditions such as heat or exercise). records.title: Cystic fibrosis related diabetes has a distinct gut microbiome dysbiosis
records.summary: Objectives: Gut microbiome dysbiosis is common across people with CF (pwCF) and may be further exacerbated with additional GI co-abnormalities. CF-related diabetes (CFRD) is a prominent comorbidity in pwCF, yet its impact upon the gut microbiome is not known. We investigated the gut microbiota, metabolome, and lipidome across pwCF with/without CFRD and control participants. Methods: Stool samples from pwCF with (n=33) and without (n=33) CFRD, alongside controls (n=19), were subjected to full-length 16S rRNA microbiota sequencing. records.title: Optimized human Precision-Cut Lung Slices (PCLS) as a translational preclinical model for cystic fibrosis
records.summary: Background and objectives: Widely used cystic fibrosis (CF) in vitro models, including cell lines, primary airway epithelial cultures and organoids are essential for testing CFTR modulators (CFTRm), but fail to capture the complexity of native lung tissue. Precision-cut lung slices (PCLS) from healthy or explanted CF lungs retain three-dimensional architecture and cellular diversity, providing a highly relevant ex vivo translational system. Our aim was to develop and validate an optimized human PCLS protocol supporting prolonged tissue viability, preservation of key structural and functional features, and reproducible response to infectious, inflammatory and pharmacological stimuli. records.title: Discriminating old from new: application of Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) to the reappearance of Pseudomonas aeruginosa in patients with cystic fibrosis
records.summary: Objectives: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (PA) is a key pathogen in cystic fibrosis (CF), linked to high morbidity and mortality. Early antibiotic therapy may eradicate new PA, but if it reappears, eradication may not have happened. Distinguishing between strain persistence and new acquisition is crucial for guiding treatment strategies to prevent chronicity. This is also important for CF patients on elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI), re-presenting PA after negativity. Using Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), we aimed to assess the clonality of PA strains, focusing on isolates responsible for 1st and 2nd infections, intermittent colonisation and re-emergence on ETI. records.summary: Objectives: People with Cystic Fibrosis (CF) are achieving successful pregnancies with CF transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) modulator therapy; predominantly elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI). records.title: A new in vivo model to assess bacterial persistence and host inflammation upon mono- or co-infection with Pseudomonas aeruginosa isolates from cystic fibrosis patients
records.summary: Objectives: P. aeruginosa is a major pathogen in cystic fibrosis (CF) patients, causing chronic lung infections associated with strong inflammation. records.title: A flexible microfluidic system accurately measures sweat chloride at point of care.
records.summary: Objectives: Quantitative pilocarpine iontophoresis sweat testing is essential for diagnosis of cystic fibrosis (CF), but requires specialised equipment and technical proficiency, limiting widespread availability and contributing to diagnostic delays, especially in less resourced areas or countries. records.summary: Objectives: In pediatric cystic fibrosis (CF) care, Swedish registry data show that mothers attend medical visits more frequently than fathers, with no indication of a narrowing gap. records.summary: Background: Emerging studies indicate that people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) have a substantially increased risk (~5-fold) of colorectal cancer (CRC) compared with the general population. Additionally, loss of CFTR function has been shown to promote partial epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT), a cellular program that may contribute to cancer progression. Objective: To elucidate how loss of CFTR impacts epithelial differentiation and promotes EMT and cancer-associated processes. Methods: Intestinal organoids (IOs) generated from healthy and CF donors were analysed for epithelial and mesenchymal markers and cell-type composition by immunofluorescence and Western blot. records.title: Changes in fat-soluble vitamin levels in children with cystic fibrosis treated with CFTR modulators
records.summary: Background: People with cystic fibrosis (CF) often require supplementation of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E) due to malabsorption. CFTR modulators (CFTRm), particularly elaxacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI), may alter vitamin absorption and metabolism by correcting the underlying CFTR defect. Aim: To evaluate changes in vitamin D, A and E levels among children with CF treated with CFTRm. Methods: Single-centre prospective registry data were analysed. Vitamin concentrations were measured at the start of CFTRm therapy (Vitamin D only) and annually thereafter. Pancreas exocrine status was assed using faecal elastase levels. records.summary: Objectives: People with cystic fibrosis have an increased risk and earlier onset of colorectal cancer (CRC). Methods: A multi-centre retrospective cohort study was conducted across three CF centers (2014–2024). records.summary: Colistin, a last-resort antibiotic against multidrug resistant Gram-negative bacteria (doi:10.1189/fcimb.2022.851784), targets the bacterial outer membrane (OM) by electrostatic interactions. Bacteria may counteract this mechanism by OM remodeling which in P. aeruginosa is mainly due to lipid A aminoarabinosylation mediated by the arn operon. Based on previous observations showing that P. aeruginosa biofilms are tolerant to colistin (doi: 10.1371/journal.ppat.1000213), we asked whether the arn operon is involved in this phenomenon. records.title: Complex CFTR alleles harbouring two cystic fibrosis–causing variants, including E831X
records.summary: Objectives: Complex CFTR alleles, defined as two or more variants in cis (i.e., on the same CF allele), complicate diagnostic evaluation and genotype–phenotype correlations and may negatively affect the response to CFTR modulator therapy. records.title: Out with the old, in with the new? A functional comparison of vanzacaftor/tezacaftor/deutivactor and elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor treatment in patient-derived intestinal organoids
records.summary: Objectives: Cystic fibrosis (CF) results from pathogenic CFTR variants that impair epithelial chloride and bicarbonate transport. CFTR modulators such as elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) have transformed CF care, and the next‑generation regimen vanzacaftor/tezacaftor/deutivacaftor (VTD) has recently been introduced on the market. VTD offers once‑daily dosing and expanded regulatory approval, yet its functional advantages remain not fully characterized across a broad set of genotypes. Consequently, we set out to compare CFTR functional rescue of ETI and VTD in primary patient-derived intestinal organoids (PDIOs) harbouring a broad range of CFTR variants. Methods: We evaluated ETI and VTD efficacy in 62 PD...

records.title: Lung function trajectories in the era of highly effective CFTR modulator therapy: analysis of the ECFS patient registry
records.summary: Objectives: Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) has been shown to improve lung function in people with CF (pwCF), with recent clinical trials suggesting sustained long-term benefits. However, pulmonary function decline remains a concern, and long-term real-world data are limited. We aimed to analyze data from the European Cystic Fibrosis Society Patient Registry (ECFSPR) to describe long-term FEV₁ trajectories following ETI initiation and to evaluate factors associated with post-treatment decline. Methods: We analyzed percent predicted FEV₁ (ppFEV₁) trajectories in adult pwCF with at least 3 years of follow-up following ETI initiation. records.title: CMTX-101, an anti-biofilm antibody, improves lung function and reduces Pseudomonas aeruginosa burden and inflammation in cystic fibrosis: results from a phase 1b/2a trial (NCT06159725)
records.summary: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Pa) is a major cause of morbidity in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) despite highly effective modulatory therapy (HEMT) and inhaled antibiotics. Bacterial biofilms increase antibiotic resistance and inflammation. CMTX-101 is a pathogen-agnostic antibody that collapses biofilms, facilitates bacterial clearance, reduces inflammation, and restores immune clearance of bacteria. This Phase 1b/2a study evaluated a single intravenous (IV) dose of CMTX-101 administered at the start of an inhaled antibiotic cycle in pwCF with chronic Pa infection. records.title: Omega 3 and omega 6 fatty acid levels in serum phospholipids did not increase after CFTR modulator treatment despite improved nutritional and pulmonary status in people with cystic fibrosis
records.summary: Objectives: Essential fatty acids have been hypothesized to play a role as a disease-modifier in cystic fibrosis (CF). We aimed to assess fatty acid-status in serum phospholipids before and after CFTR modulator (CFTRm) therapy. Methods: The National Swedish CF registry was used to retrieve data on serum phospholipids (in mol%) before and after the initiation of CFTRm treatment in children (n=92) and adults (n=119) with CF followed at Stockholm CF Center (elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor: n=104, lumacaftor/ivacaftor: n=86, ivacaftor: n=8, tezacaftor/ivacaftor: n=10, other: n=3). records.title: A phase 3, 24-week, open-label study of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor in children with cystic fibrosis 12 to <24 months of age
records.summary: Objectives: Assess safety, pharmacokinetics (PK) and efficacy of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ELX/TEZ/IVA) in children with CF aged 12-<24 months with ≥1 F508del or ELX/TEZ/IVA-responsive CFTR allele. Methods: This 24-week, phase 3, open-label study of ELX/TEZ/IVA enrolled children aged 12-<24 months with responsive genotypes, including F/MF and F/F. Primary endpoint was safety and tolerability. Secondary endpoints were PK and absolute change in sweat chloride. Results: 54 children enrolled and received ELX/TEZ/IVA. 53 children (98.1%) had adverse events (AE); majority were mild (42.6%) or moderate (46.3%). 6 children (11.1%) had serious AEs, none considered related to ELX/TEZ/IVA. records.summary: Objectives: To determine the 12-month cost-effectiveness of reducing treatment burden by discontinuing nebulised muco-active therapies in people with cystic fibrosis (CF) receiving ETI. Methods: CF STORM (ISRCTN=14081521) was a multicentre, registry-based randomised controlled trial enrolling people with CF aged ≥6 years established on ETI and using at least one daily nebulised muco-active therapy. Participants were randomised 1:1 to STOP or CONTINUE their muco-active therapy (dornase alfa, hypertonic saline, or both). records.title: Finding the Optimal Regimen for Mycobacterium abscessus Treatment (FORMaT) trial: first interim analysis of 60 intervention participants after short intensive therapy
records.summary: Objectives: Treatment for Mycobacterium abscessus pulmonary disease (MABSC-PD) is characterised by prolonged use of multiple, expensive antibiotics that exhibit substantial toxicity and poor effectiveness. The Finding the Optimal Regimen for Mycobacterium abscessus Treatment (FORMaT) adaptive platform trial (APT) aims to find the best treatment for MABSC-PD. There is an intensive treatment phase using intravenous and oral antimicrobials followed by a consolidation phase each with separate randomisations. An Observational Cohort (OC) simultaneously recruits participants with positive cultures who do not meet criteria for treatment. records.title: Association between parameters of body composition and lipid profile in adults with cystic fibrosis
records.summary: Background: Advances in CF care and the widespread use of highly effective modulator therapies (HEMT) have shifted the nutritional profile of adults with CF, with rising prevalence of overweight, obesity and altered body composition. The cardiometabolic implications of increased adiposity on lipid profiles remains unclear. Objectives: To characterise body composition and lipid profiles in adults with CF, and to examine how Body Mass Index (BMI), and fat mass percentage (FM%) relate to atherogenic lipid profiles. Methods: This cross-sectional retrospective chart review was conducted at a single UK adult CF centre. records.title: Antibiotic use remains key to recovery from acute lung function declines in the highly effective modulator era
records.summary: Background: Fewer pulmonary exacerbations are being diagnosed in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) since the introduction of the highly effective modulator therapy (HEMT) elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI). We assessed current antibiotic (ABX) treatment responses to acute drops in FEV1 and their effect in the post ETI era. Methods: Using CF Foundation Patient Registry data (2021–2023), we identified the first relative decline from baseline of 5%-50% in PwCF aged 6–40 years with baseline FEV1pp >50%, stratified by consistent vs no HEMT use. records.title: Longitudinal trends in nutritional status and associations with lung function in the paediatric population with cystic fibrosis: data from the UK CF Registry
records.summary: Introduction: Optimising nutritional status (NS) remains a key priority for children with CF (cwCF). Reports on NS trends in the UK are limited. The relationship between BMI and lung function in the CFTR modulator era remains unclear. Aim: To describe longitudinal trends in NS category in UK cwCF and associations of BMI and BMI change with clinical outcomes by modulator use. Method: Data on all cwCF aged 2–17 years recorded in the UK CF Registry between 2007-2023 were analysed. CwCF were categorised by BMI z score following ESPEN-ESPGHAN-ECFS 2024 definitions; underweight, target range, overweight, and obese. records.title: Global consensus-based guidance for the nutritional management of children with cystic fibrosis (cwCF) on elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI)
records.summary: Objectives: ETI has significantly altered the nutritional trajectory of cwCF. Rollout has outpaced the development of nutritional guidance. This study aimed to: 1. systematically review the literature on ETI and nutrition in cwCF 2. develop expert consensus-based guidance 3. highlight research gaps. Methods: This project was registered on PROSPERO (ID: 587618). A global panel of 22 dietitians, gastroenterologists, respirologists, endocrinologists, and a parent representative was convened. records.summary: Objectives: Pregnancies among people with cystic fibrosis (PwCF) have increased in the CFTR modulator (CFTRm) era. Follow-up practices for exposed children vary, and no widely accepted recommendations exist. We aimed to develop the first international recommendations for clinical follow-up of children exposed to CFTRm in utero and/or via breastfeeding. records.title: Optimizing clinical outcomes after meconium ileus with low dose off-label elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor after surgical intervention: a retrospective study
records.summary: Objectives: Meconium ileus represents one of the most severe neonatal manifestations of cystic fibrosis (CF) and is associated with substantial morbidity. Surgical intervention, including procedures such as ileostomy and end-to-end anastomosis, is frequently required and is followed by an increased risk of gastro-intestinal complications. This study aimed to evaluate whether early off-label administration of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) is associated with improved post-operative clinical and gastro-intestinal outcomes. Methods: A retrospective cohort study was conducted including children with CF treated surgically for meconium ileus at Wilhelmina Children's Hospital between 2007 and 2025. Four patie...

records.title: Interrogating the molecular basis for inter-donor variation in in-vitro functional responses to modulators amongst individuals homozygous for F508del​
records.summary: Cystic Fibrosis Transmembrane Regulator (CFTR) functional response to modulators observed in-vitro in primary Human Nasal Epithelial Cells (HNECs) are widely used to predict clinical outcomes and guide access to CFTR modulators. However, there is heterogeneity observed in the CFTR responses measured in the HNEC model even between individuals carrying the same genotype such as F508del/F508del and the basis of this heterogeneity is unknown. We propose that this variability primarily reflects patient-specific tissue properties rather than technical error. records.title: Improved ventilation homogeneity after step-down of inhalation and airway clearance therapy in children with cystic fibrosis receiving elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor
records.summary: Objectives: Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) has markedly improved lung function in people with cystic fibrosis (CF), raising questions about the need for twice-daily inhalation and airway clearance therapy. This study evaluated the effects of reducing inhalation and airway clearance from twice to once daily in children with CF receiving ETI. Methods: Children aged 6–17 years at the paediatric CF centres in Gothenburg and Uppsala, treated with ETI for ≥9 months and performing inhalation and airway clearance twice daily, were offered reduction to once daily for 12 months when clinically stable. Outcomes included spirometry, lung clearance index (LCI), airway microbiology, antibiotic use, and respiratory s...

records.title: Developing a consistent framework for psychological screening of cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) modulator side effects in adults: a UK multicentre approach
records.summary: Objectives: Since the introduction of CFTR modulators, there have been increasing reports of psychological and neurocognitive side effects, particularly with elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI). A UK drug safety update in May 2025 highlighted infrequent reports of psychological side effects including anxiety, low mood, sleep disturbance, poor concentration, and forgetfulness. While these risks have been formally documented for ETI, they may also apply to other modulators. The European Cystic Fibrosis Society (ECFS) Standards of Care recommend screening in accordance with CF mental health guidelines prior to and within three months of commencing modulators. The UK rollout of vanzacaftor/tezacaftor/deutivacaf...

records.title: ETI reshapes aerobic trajectories in cystic fibrosis: lung function improves, exercise capacity tells a more complex story
records.summary: Elexacaftor–tezacaftor–ivacaftor (ETI) improves lung outcomes in cystic fibrosis (CF), but its effect on functional status and aerobic capacity remains unclear, as evidence is scarce, mostly from small, short-term, or paediatric cohorts. Cardiopulmonary exercise testing (CPET) with maximal oxygen uptake (VO₂ max) measurement is the gold standard to assess aerobic capacity. Objectives: We aimed to track longitudinal changes in aerobic capacity after ETI initiation in paediatric and adult CFwith patients (pwCF) and identify key determinants of VO₂ max, including lung function and muscular conditioning. records.title: Abnormalities of the distal terminal ileum diameter in people with cystic fibrosis assessed using magnetic resonance imaging
records.summary: Objective: The terminal ileum (TI) is commonly impacted in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF), with earlier surgical reports describing a dilated TI, filled with clay-like material. However, quantitative data on TI dimensions in pwCF is limited. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) enables non-invasive assessment of the small bowel morphology, though no data on TI diameters in pwCF is available. This study aimed to: (1) measure the diameter of the distal TI in pwCF and healthy volunteers (HVs) using MRI; (2) test the hypothesis that the TI diameter is increased in pwCF compared to HVs. Methods: MRI imaging of the TI was completed in 26 adult pwCF (23 on CFTR modulator therapy) and 30 adult HVs. A commercial image...

records.title: Assessing CFTR modulator efficacy via urine bicarbonate excretion following a short therapy interruption
records.summary: Objectives: Renal CFTR function is essential for urinary HCO3- excretion and is markedly impaired in people with CF (pwCF). Urinary HCO3- excretion following an oral NaHCO3 load may serve as a simple in vivo biomarker of CFTR function, reflecting both the extent of CFTR dysfunction and therapy-induced rescue. This study investigates challenged urine HCO3- excretion in pwCF treated with elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI), before, during, and after a short therapy interruption, and examines the association with venous acid-base parameters and ETI plasma concentrations. records.title: Persistent decline in FEV1 in adults with cystic fibrosis treated with elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor
records.summary: Objectives: Without CFTR modulators, cystic fibrosis (CF) is associated with an accelerated decline in percent predicted forced expiratory volume in one second (ppFEV1), at a rate of approximately -1.5% per year. Although treatment with elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor (ETI) initially increases ppFEV1, little is known about its impact on subsequent FEV1 decline. Recent analyses of the extension phases of the Phase 3 ETI pivotal studies (Daines et al., AJRCCM, 2025) found no evidence of ppFEV1 decline over four years in patients aged 12 and older who were treated with ETI. records.title: Clinician experience implementing cystic fibrosis-specific cognitive behavioral therapy (CF-CBT) in the US and Canada
records.summary: Objectives: CF-CBT is an 8-session intervention to address depression and anxiety in adults with CF (AWCF). Between 2022-25, >200 US and Canadian CF clinicians completed an 8-hour CF-CBT training. We conducted a focus group examining clinician experiences of CF-CBT implementation as part of a mixed-methods randomized implementation trial. Methods: Nine CF-CBT trained clinicians participated in a focus group in July 2025: 8 (89%) women, 7 (78%) white, 8 (89%) heterosexual; 7 (78%) social workers, 1 psychologist, 1 nurse practitioner; working in CF 0-17 years. records.title: Beyond exercise testing: linking exercise capacity, enjoyment and participation among people with cystic fibrosis
records.summary: Objectives: To investigate the relationship between peak exercise capacity and enjoyment of, and motivation for, physical activity (PA) and exercise participation (EP) in people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF). Methods: 56 pwCF (31f/26m, age 18.3±10.5yrs, ppFEV1 84.8±16.9,) participated. A cycle-ergometry was performed to determine VO2peak and Wpeak. German versions of the Physical Activity Enjoyment Scale (PACES-D) was used to assess PA and EP enjoyment, and the Behavioural Regulation in Exercise Questionnaire Version 2 (BREQ-2-D) assessed motivational aspects. records.title: Effects of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor initiation in advance cystic fibrosis-related liver disease in the Netherlands
records.summary: Objectives: Elexacaftor–tezacaftor–ivacaftor (ETI) was recently introduced in the Netherlands, including for persons with advanced cystic fibrosis-related liver disease (aCFLD). This retrospective study examined the potential therapeutic and hepatotoxic effects of ETI in persons with aCFLD. Methods: We compared persons with aCFLD and those without CF-related hepatic involvement (noCFHBI) upon ETI initiation and up to 12 months thereafter, adjusting for prior CFTR modulator use. Using the Dutch Cystic Fibrosis Foundation registry, we analysed between the two groups the liver enzyme levels and fibrosis indices (APRI and GPR) during the 12 months study period. records.title: Shared MSD2 binding site but distinct potency of elexacaftor and vanzacaftor in correcting F508del-CFTR
records.summary: Many individuals with cystic fibrosis (CF) benefit from Trikafta™, a triple CFTR modulator composed of the correctors: elexacaftor (VX-445) and tezacaftor (VX-661), and the potentiator: ivacaftor (VX-770), a.k.a. ETI). In cryo-EM studies, the two correctors have been shown to bind to two different regions on the F508del-CFTR to act in an additive or synergistic manner to induce near-Wt assembly via allostery. ETI treatment has been found to improve lung function in patients with at least one F508del-CFTR mutation. However, a subset shows limited benefit or adverse effects. records.summary: Background & Objectives: We demonstrated (Liessi JCF 2023, Ciobanu JCF 2024) that Tezacaftor inhibits the enzyme (DEGS) which converts dihydroceramides (dHCer) into ceramides (Fabrias Prog.Lip.Res. 2012), thus producing increase of dHCer in cells. Since DEGS malfunctioning is often associated with severe neurological impairments linked with aberrant myelin composition and structure (Karsai JCI 2019, Pant JCI 2019), we conducted an in vivo drug safety study, by administering ETI to CD-1 mice during pregnancy and breastfeeding. records.title: The impact of progressive expansion of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor availability on lung transplant for cystic fibrosis in France
records.summary: Objectives: Several countries reported a reduction in lung transplants for people with cystic fibrosis (pwCF) after elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor (ETI) was introduced. We sought to evaluate how lung transplant indications evolved in relation to CFTR genotypes and the use of ETI. Methods: All lung transplants performed on pwCF in France between 2015 and 2024 were identified using data from the French Agence de la Biomédecine. Individual-level patient data, including CFTR genotypes and use of ETI at the time of transplant, were obtained from the French CF reference network centers.
